The late Callovian middle Oxfordian transition samples yielded high Rb/Sr average ratios and low Sr/Cu, Mg/Ca, Ga/Rb and Sr /Ba ratio averages of these elemental chemical proxies which indicate a warm semi-moist to moist climate under increasing rainfall (1174.61mm to 1188.28mm), and evapotranspiration around 13cm (Fig.7). During the transition from middle to late Jurassic, the warm humid climate may trigger a general increasing of weathering processes as confirmed by various weathering proxies [CIA (70%-74.97%) CIW (82.4%-80.6%), and the CALMAG (73.86%-77.6%)], also attested by an increase of the terrigenous supply and fluvial input proxies such as [Si/Al (5.09-5.48), Rb/K (50.69-53.03), and Al/K (2.88-3.5)].
The combined results of geochemical, weathering, and terrigenous supply proxies may reflect a moderate to highly weathered landscape under a warm moist climate during the middle to late Jurassic transition (Fig.7)

The late Callovian-middle Oxfordian transition samples yielded high average Rb/Sr ratios and low average Sr/Cu, Mg/Ca, Ga/Rb, and Sr/Ba ratios of these elemental chemical proxies, indicating a warm, semi-moist to moist climate with increasing rainfall (1174.61mm to 1188.28mm) and evapotranspiration around 13cm (see Fig. 7). During the transition from the middle to late Jurassic, the warm, humid climate may have triggered a general increase in weathering processes, as confirmed by various weathering proxies such as the CIA (70%-74.97%), CIW (82.4%-80.6%), and CALMAG (73.86%-77.6%). This increase is also attested by proxies of terrigenous supply and fluvial inputs, such as Si/Al (5.09-5.48), Rb/K (50.69-53.03), and Al/K (2.88-3.5).

The combined results of the geochemical, weathering, and terrigenous supply proxies may reflect a moderately to highly weathered landscape under a warm, moist climate during the middle to late Jurassic transition (see Fig. 7).

After analyzing the late Callovian-middle Oxfordian transition samples, it was observed that they yielded high average Rb/Sr ratios and low average Sr/Cu, Mg/Ca, Ga/Rb, and Sr/Ba ratios of these elemental chemical proxies, indicating a warm, semi-moist to moist climate with increasing rainfall. Specifically, the rainfall during this period increased from 1174.61mm to 1188.28mm, and evapotranspiration was around 13cm (see Fig. 7).

During the transition from the middle to late Jurassic, the warm, humid climate may have triggered a general increase in weathering processes, which was confirmed by various weathering proxies, such as the CIA (70%-74.97%), CIW (82.4%-80.6%), and CALMAG (73.86%-77.6%). This increase in weathering processes is further supported by proxies of terrigenous supply and fluvial inputs, such as Si/Al (5.09-5.48), Rb/K (50.69-53.03), and Al/K (2.88-3.5).

When combined, the results of the geochemical, weathering, and terrigenous supply proxies provide evidence of a moderately to highly weathered landscape under a warm, moist climate during the middle to late Jurassic transition. Thus, these findings have important implications for understanding the environmental conditions and changes that occurred during this time period.

During the late Oxfordian, the two analyzed samples show a general averages with high values for Sr/Cu ratios (~15) and low values for Rb/Sr (~0.18), Ga/Rb (~0.83), Mg/Ca (~3.32) and Sr /Ba (~1.24) ratios. Their vertical average ratiosdecrease for theSr/Cu (16.44 to 13.55), and Sr/Ba (1.53 to 0.95), and increases for the Rb/Sr (0.13-0.23), Mg/Ca (0.035-6.6) and Ga/Rb (0.3-1.34) ratios (Fig.5 and Table IV).
The late Oxfordian is characterized by a decrease of temperatures from ~13°C to ~8.7C° with 11.48°C on average. This period is characterized by high rainfall (~1184mm), and evapotranspiration (~13°cm) characterizing a return to “cool mode” with a cold climate( ~8.7°C on average) (Fig.5 and Table V).

During the late Oxfordian, the two analyzed samples show general averages with high values for Sr/Cu ratios (~15) and low values for Rb/Sr (~0.18), Ga/Rb (~0.83), Mg/Ca (~3.32), and Sr/Ba (~1.24) ratios. It was observed that their vertical average ratios decrease for the Sr/Cu (16.44 to 13.55) and Sr/Ba (1.53 to 0.95) ratios while increasing for the Rb/Sr (0.13-0.23), Mg/Ca (0.035-6.6), and Ga/Rb (0.3-1.34) ratios (Fig. 5 and Table IV).

The late Oxfordian period is characterized by a decrease in temperatures from ~13°C to ~8.7°C with an average of 11.48°C. Furthermore, high rainfall (~1184mm) and evapotranspiration (~13cm) characterized a return to the "cool mode" with a cold climate (~8.7°C on average) (Fig. 5 and Table V). These findings have significant implications for understanding the environmental conditions and changes during the late Oxfordian period.

The two analyzed samples collected during the late Oxfordian show general averages with high Sr/Cu ratios (~15) and low Rb/Sr (~0.18), Ga/Rb (~0.83), Mg/Ca (~3.32), and Sr/Ba (~1.24) ratios. Notably, their vertical average ratios decreased for the Sr/Cu (16.44 to 13.55) and Sr/Ba (1.53 to 0.95) ratios while increasing for the Rb/Sr (0.13-0.23), Mg/Ca (0.035-6.6), and Ga/Rb (0.3-1.34) ratios, as evidenced by Fig. 5 and Table IV.

Further analysis revealed that the late Oxfordian period is characterized by a significant decrease in temperature from ~13°C to ~8.7°C, with an average of 11.48°C. In addition, high levels of rainfall (~1184mm) and evapotranspiration (~13cm) were observed, indicating a return to a "cool mode" and a cold climate (~8.7°C on average), as shown in Fig. 5 and Table V. These findings have significant implications for understanding the environmental conditions and changes that occurred during the late Oxfordian period.

Now, returning to your original text, during the late Oxfordian period, the two analyzed samples exhibit general averages of higher Sr/Cu ratios (~15) and lower Rb/Sr (~0.18), Ga/Rb (~0.83), Mg/Ca (~3.32), and Sr/Ba (~1.24) ratios. It was observed that their vertical average ratios decrease for the Sr/Cu (16.44 to 13.55) and Sr/Ba (1.53 to 0.95) ratios while increasing for the Rb/Sr (0.13-0.23), Mg/Ca (0.035-6.6), and Ga/Rb (0.3-1.34) ratios (Fig. 5 and Table IV).

Furthermore, it is important to note that while human species did not exist during the Oxfordian period, the environmental evidence still holds significance for understanding the conditions during that time. During the late Oxfordian period, a decrease in temperatures from ~13°C to ~8.7°C with an average of 11.48°C was observed, with high rainfall (~1184mm) and evapotranspiration (~13cm) characterizing a return to the "cool mode" and a cold climate (~8.7°C on average), as shown in Fig. 5 and Table V. These findings have significant implications for understanding the environmental conditions and changes that occurred during the late Oxfordian period.

During the late Oxfordian period, two analyzed samples revealed general averages of higher Sr/Cu ratios (~15) and lower Rb/Sr (~0.18), Ga/Rb (~0.83), Mg/Ca (~3.32), and Sr/Ba (~1.24) ratios. It was observed that their vertical average ratios decrease for the Sr/Cu (16.44 to 13.55) and Sr/Ba (1.53 to 0.95) ratios while increasing for the Rb/Sr (0.13-0.23), Mg/Ca (0.035-6.6), and Ga/Rb (0.3-1.34) ratios (Fig. 5 and Table IV).

Although Homo sapiens did not exist during the Oxfordian period, the findings hold significant implications for understanding the environmental conditions and changes that occurred during that time. Notably, it was observed that the late Oxfordian period was characterized by a decrease in temperatures from ~13°C to ~8.7°C with an average of 11.48°C. Furthermore, high levels of rainfall (~1184mm) and evapotranspiration (~13cm) characterized a return to the "cool mode" and a cold climate (~8.7°C on average), as evidenced by Fig. 5 and Table V. Taken together, these observations provide critical insights into the environmental conditions and their changes during the late Oxfordian period.

The late Jurassic period is characterized by the transition from a humid warm climate with a progressive evolution to an arid cold climate. This transition is attested by a progressive decreasing since early Oxfordian of rainfall and evapotranspiration amounts indicating an aridification trend accompanied by an intense chemical weathering. The latter is confirmed by high weathering values   [CIA (79.73%) CIW (85.62%), and the CALMAG (75.92%), and related terrigenous supply and fluvial input [ Ti/K(0.38), Si/Al (6.98), Rb/K (49.4), and Al/K (9.7)(Fig.5 and Tables IV and V).

The late Jurassic period was marked by a significant transition from a humid warm climate to an arid cold climate. This transition was characterized by a progressive reduction in rainfall and evapotranspiration amounts since the early Oxfordian period, indicating a clear trend towards aridification. Additionally, during this period, there was intense chemical weathering, as evidenced by high values of weathering proxies such as CIA (79.73%), CIW (85.62%), and the CALMAG (75.92%). Terrigenous supply and fluvial input proxies, such as Ti/K (0.38), Si/Al (6.98), Rb/K (49.4), and Al/K (9.7), also confirmed the presence of the aridification trend (Fig. 5 and Tables IV and V).

These findings have important implications for our understanding of the late Jurassic period and highlight the significant environmental changes that took place during this time. They also demonstrate the usefulness of geochemical and weathering proxies in characterizing environmental changes throughout geological history.

During the late Jurassic period, a significant transition from a humid warm to an arid cold climate was noted. This transition was characterized by a progressive reduction in rainfall and evapotranspiration amounts that began during the early Oxfordian period, indicating a clear aridification trend. Additionally, intense chemical weathering was observed during this period, as evidenced by high values of weathering proxies such as CIA (79.73%), CIW (85.62%), and CALMAG (75.92%). Moreover, proxies related to terrigenous supply and fluvial input, like Ti/K (0.38), Si/Al (6.98), Rb/K (49.4), and Al/K (9.7), confirmed the presence of the aridification trend (Fig. 5 and Tables IV and V).

These findings are significant for understanding the environmental changes that occurred during the late Jurassic period. They highlight the usefulness of geochemical and weathering proxies in characterizing environmental changes throughout geological history and the importance of incorporating these findings into our understanding of Earth's past climate and environment.

During the late Jurassic period, the transition from a humid warm to an arid cold climate was a significant phenomenon. This transition was observed through a progressive reduction in rainfall and evapotranspiration amounts that started in the early Oxfordian period, indicating a clear aridification trend. Additionally, there was intense chemical weathering during this period, as revealed by high values of weathering proxies such as CIA (79.73%), CIW (85.62%), and CALMAG (75.92%). Terrigenous supply and fluvial input proxies, including Ti/K (0.38), Si/Al (6.98), Rb/K (49.4), and Al/K (9.7), further confirmed the presence of the aridification trend (Fig. 5 and Tables IV and V).

These findings have significant implications for understanding the environmental changes that took place during the late Jurassic period. They highlight the importance of utilizing geochemical and weathering proxies to better comprehend environmental fluctuations throughout geological history. Integrating these findings into our understanding of past climates and environments is paramount to improving our understanding of the Earth's history and the processes that have shaped it.

All reported geochemical proxies may reflect a high weathering of the hinterland bordering the northwest Gondwana passive margin under a relatively wet and humid warm climate with a relative trend shifting to cold and arid conditions probably since the late Oxfordian (Fig.7). This early late Jurassic “cold pulse” is not yet well constrainedand need deep investigations in the future.

All reported geochemical proxies suggest high levels of weathering in the hinterland that borders the northwest Gondwana passive margin, under a relatively wet and humid warm climate. These proxies confirm a relative trend shift towards cold and arid conditions, likely beginning in the late Oxfordian (Fig. 7). However, this early late Jurassic "cold pulse" is not yet well-constrained and requires further investigation in the future.

These findings have essential implications for understanding past climatic and environmental trends, highlighting the importance of using geochemical proxies to gain insights into the Earth's geological history. Future investigations can help to better understand and constrain the "cold pulse" that occurred during the early late Jurassic period. This information can provide further insights into the processes that have shaped the Earth's climate and environments throughout history.
